Web frontends are routinely paired with App Service plans, Application Insights, network security groups and virtual networks: multiple MFIs include microsoft.web/serverfarms + microsoft.web/sites + microsoft.insights/components + microsoft.network/networksecuritygroups + microsoft.network/virtualnetworks and counts show large inventories of serverfarms (177) and App Insights (549), so plan your finance web app as an App Service in a dedicated App Service Plan with Application Insights connected to a Log Analytics workspace and place the app behind VNet integration and subnet-level NSGs for monitoring and network segmentation.

Key Vaults and user-assigned managed identities are a common secret/access pattern for apps and storage: tenant data shows 169 Key Vaults and 370 managed identities and many MFIs repeatedly co-locate microsoft.keyvault/vaults with microsoft.managedidentity/userassignedidentities and app resources, so use a Key Vault for DB credentials and certificates accessed via a user-assigned managed identity and consider enabling Key Vault RBAC, soft-delete and purge-protection to harden the finance app's secret posture.

Private connectivity is an established approach for securing platform endpoints: a medium-support MFI includes networksecuritygroups, privatednszones, privateendpoints, virtualnetworks and web/serverfarms (count 20), indicating private endpoint patterns are used, so design the finance relational backend (Azure SQL) and Key Vault with private endpoints and corresponding Private DNS zones and NSG rules to prevent public exposure.

Existing Azure SQL databases in the tenant trend toward vCore/Hyperscale Gen5 SKUs: database SKU aggregations show HS_Gen5 and Hyperscale are prominent (Hyperscale 60% and HS_Gen5 40% among reported databases), so for the finance relational backend evaluate a vCore-based Hyperscale or GeneralPurpose (Gen5) configuration sized to workload and include private endpoint and backup/DR choices aligned with your RPO/RTO requirements.

WAF-capable gateways are the tenant standard in front of web apps: all reported application gateways use WAF_v2 and CDN profiles use Premium_AzureFrontDoor, and MFIs pair applicationgateways + wafpolicies + NSGs + public IPs, so include a WAF (Application Gateway WAF_v2 or Azure Front Door) at the edge for the finance app and keep backend connectivity internal to the VNet while exposing only the edge public IP.

Monitoring and alerting follow a consistent pattern of App Insights + Log Analytics + metric alerts: many MFIs combine microsoft.insights/components with microsoft.operationalinsights/workspaces and microsoft.insights/metricalerts, so provision Application Insights linked to a Log Analytics workspace and define metric and log alerts (datacollection rules) during design to match tenant operational conventions.

Storage choices favor cost-optimized local redundancy but some groups use geo-redundancy and permissive network access: storage accounts show Standard_LRS (48.5%) with appreciable Standard_GRS (23.3%) and Standard_RAGRS (17.2%) and networkacls.defaultaction Allow (92.1%), so decide storage redundancy based on finance data durability needs (LRS to save cost, GRS/RA-GRS for cross-region resiliency) and harden storage network access with network ACLs or private endpoints for sensitive files.

Network segmentation via NSGs and minimized backend public IPs is a common pattern for compute and web workloads: MFIs repeatedly include networksecuritygroups with networkinterfaces, publicipaddresses and virtualnetworks and networksecuritygroups inventory is large (957), so adopt subnet-level NSGs for app, database and gateway subnets and avoid assigning public IPs directly to backend services when planning the finance application.
